claim_text: MCI patients who convert to AD show a distinct structural-MRI signature in their frozen embeddings versus non-converters.
head: linear probe

site_scanner.detail: scanner predicted as well as outcome (margin -0.08 [95% CI -0.14, -0.02]); likely acquisition artifact

brain_age.detail: brain-age R2=0.87, MAE=1.6yr; effect retained 104% after brain-age-GAP control (Franke/Gaser standard), 0% under the stricter predicted-brain-age control

biomarker_anchor.detail: p-tau217 correlation r=-0.09 (95% CI lower -0.30, n=79) [SYNTHETIC HARNESS: calibration target, not measured plasma]

stats.provenance: SYNTHETIC HARNESS — p-tau217/GFAP are calibration targets (calibration.CAL), not measured plasma

caveats: Biomarker anchor failed: no molecular correlate found on the complete subset — treat as unanchored (blocks promotion).

SYNTHETIC HARNESS: the p-tau217 / GFAP anchor is a CALIBRATION TARGET (drawn to sit inside a literature range in calibration.CAL), not a measured plasma value — no open cohort pairs MRI with plasma markers. The molecular anchor demonstrates the gate MECHANIC, not a real result.
p-tau217 measured on a partial subset (~45% missing in a realistic cohort) — anchor is suggestive, not confirmatory.
'Partially robust' is not 'robust' — survives some but not all challenges.
Verdict is provisional and conditional on the scanner/site distribution of any replication cohort.
Brain-age adjustment uses a proxy control; residual aging signal cannot be excluded.
Any biomarker anchor is complete-case only and may not generalize.
Leakage is bounded, not eliminated — see the published batch-effect audits (‘Batch Effects in Brain Foundation Model Embeddings,’ arXiv:2604.14441 (2026)).
‘Partially robust’ is not ‘robust’. The score renormalizes over the tests that ran, so a middling verdict can hide a dimension that quietly failed — read the per-test results, not just the headline.

The brain-age control is a proxy, not a gold standard: it is embedding-derived (this cohort's fit: R2=0.87, MAE=1.6yr), so residual generic-aging signal can survive the adjustment and masquerade as disease-specific.
The biomarker anchor likely rests on a partial complete-case subset; with realistic p-tau217 missingness the correlation is estimated on far fewer subjects than the headline cohort.
N is small (~137); at this size AUCs are noisy and a single site's idiosyncrasy can dominate — treat effect sizes as wide-interval estimates.

The leakage test audits the embeddings with the same linear-probe family it uses for the outcome, so it bounds — but cannot fully rule out — shared confounding; this is the mechanic quantified in ‘Pretrained, Frozen, Still Leaking: Auditing Cross-Encoder Attribute Transfer in EEG Foundation Models’ (arXiv:2606.09189 (2026)), which we cite rather than claim.

evidence_ledger.detail: scanner predicted as well as outcome (margin -0.08 [95% CI -0.14, -0.02]); likely acquisition artifact
brain-age R2=0.87, MAE=1.6yr; effect retained 104% after brain-age-GAP control (Franke/Gaser standard), 0% under the stricter predicted-brain-age control
p-tau217 correlation r=-0.09 (95% CI lower -0.30, n=79) [SYNTHETIC HARNESS: calibration target, not measured plasma]

narration: The claim “MCI patients who convert to AD show a distinct structural-MRI signature in their frozen embeddings versus non-converters.” lands at “partially robust” (40/100). The naive effect (AUC 0.87 separating MCI converters from MCI non-converters) is real on paper, but the referee's job is to ask what else could produce it. It sits below the promotion floor, so the honest read is that acquisition, demographics or generic aging can plausibly account for it, and it should not consume a scientist's quarter without stronger evidence. Where it stops being true: that the scanner/site mix in any new cohort matches this one — the leakage margin is only -0.08 AUC, so a different scanner distribution could erase the effect. That the same frozen embeddings can predict scanner/site as well as outcome is documented prior art (“Batch Effects in Brain Foundation Model Embeddings,” arXiv:2604.14441 (2026)); NeuroAD Discovery Engine only runs the audit and issues the verdict.

critique: ‘Partially robust’ is not ‘robust’. The score renormalizes over the tests that ran, so a middling verdict can hide a dimension that quietly failed — read the per-test results, not just the headline.

The brain-age control is a proxy, not a gold standard: it is embedding-derived (this cohort's fit: R2=0.87, MAE=1.6yr), so residual generic-aging signal can survive the adjustment and masquerade as disease-specific.
The biomarker anchor likely rests on a partial complete-case subset; with realistic p-tau217 missingness the correlation is estimated on far fewer subjects than the headline cohort.
N is small (~137); at this size AUCs are noisy and a single site's idiosyncrasy can dominate — treat effect sizes as wide-interval estimates.

The leakage test audits the embeddings with the same linear-probe family it uses for the outcome, so it bounds — but cannot fully rule out — shared confounding; this is the mechanic quantified in ‘Pretrained, Frozen, Still Leaking: Auditing Cross-Encoder Attribute Transfer in EEG Foundation Models’ (arXiv:2606.09189 (2026)), which we cite rather than claim.
revised_caveats: Verdict is provisional and conditional on the scanner/site distribution of any replication cohort.
Brain-age adjustment uses a proxy control; residual aging signal cannot be excluded.
Any biomarker anchor is complete-case only and may not generalize.
Leakage is bounded, not eliminated — see the published batch-effect audits (‘Batch Effects in Brain Foundation Model Embeddings,’ arXiv:2604.14441 (2026)).
